summary:
The Tale of Genji (Genji Monogatari, c. 1008 AD) by Murasaki Shikibu, a lady-in-waiting at the [[event:2164|Heian]] imperial court, is widely regarded as the world's first novel — a work of psychological depth, literary sophistication, and narrative complexity that rivals the greatest fiction of any era. Following the romantic and political life of Hikaru Genji, the brilliant but melancholy son of a Japanese emperor, across 54 chapters and decades, the novel explores aristocratic court society with acute observation, multiple narrative perspectives, and a pervasive aesthetic of mono no aware — the bittersweet consciousness of transience that defines the Heian sensibility.

Murasaki Shikibu wrote in hiragana (the syllabic script developed for Japanese vernacular writing), which was the medium of educated women's expression in an era when formal Chinese literacy was reserved for men. The novel's sophisticated characterization — Genji and dozens of other characters are rendered with individual psychologies and complex motivations that feel modern — its sustained narrative across multiple generations, and its aesthetic richness place it in a different category from earlier prose fiction. The Tale of Genji has been continuously read, illustrated (the Genji scrolls, c. 1120 AD, are the oldest surviving Japanese narrative paintings), and adapted since its composition. Its influence on Japanese aesthetic culture is comparable to Homer's influence on Greek culture — it defined ideals of beauty, emotion, and social grace that shaped Japanese aristocratic and later popular culture for a millennium.
